text: The surgeon performs an isolated caudate lobe resection, removes a tumor from the inferior vena cava, conducts a right adrenalectomy, and dissects aortocaval lymph nodes.
A patient was recurrent caudate lobe hepatocellular carcinoma, inferior vena cava tumor thrombosis and aortocaval lymph node metastases after bisegmentectomy of segment V and VI.
The surgeon begins by mobilizing the liver and lysing adhesions, carefully separating the liver from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon explores the caudate lobe, visually assessing the anatomical structures and surrounding tissues for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon performs retro-hepatic dissection of the inferior vena cava from the caudate lobe of the liver, carefully separating the vascular structure from the surrounding liver tissue.
The surgeon applies the Pringle maneuver, temporarily occluding the hepatic artery and portal vein to control blood flow during the liver resection.
The surgeon performs liver parenchymal transection, carefully dividing the liver tissue along the planned resection line.
Intraoperative ultrasonography reveals the presence of a tumor thrombus within the inferior vena cava (IVC), visible on the ultrasound screen.
The surgeon performs total hepatic vascular exclusion, temporarily occluding blood flow to the liver to facilitate safe resection.
The surgeon applies the Pringle's maneuver, occluding the hepatoduodenal ligament to control blood flow to the liver during the procedure.
The surgeon applies proximal and distal clamps on the inferior vena cava (IVC) to control blood flow during the liver resection procedure.
The surgeon performs a covotomy and partially resects the wall of the inferior vena cava (IVC) to remove the tumor located in the caudate lobe of the liver.
The surgeon repairs the inferior vena cava using a continuous running suture technique with Prolene suture material.
The surgeon begins an auto-caval lymph node dissection from the retrocaval area, carefully isolating lymphatic tissue adjacent to the inferior vena cava.
The surgeon performs a right adrenalectomy using a bipolar sealing device, carefully excising the adrenal gland from its surrounding tissue.
pathological confirmed hepatocellular carcinoma, IVC tumor thrombus and lymph node metastases.
